Land Rover Discovery 2.7 TDV6 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Discovery 2.7 TDV6 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 276DT engine typically costs £5,000–£7,500 supply only, with used units available from £2,800–£4,500. Fitting adds £1,800–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Discovery.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Discovery 2.7 TDV6 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's a robust engine with one major caveat: the oil pump housing. Pre-2010 vehicles are the highest risk; post-2012 Discovery 4 examples with the revised oil pump design are significantly safer. With preventative oil pump work and good maintenance, these engines are capable of high mileage.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Land Rover Discovery 2.7 TDV6?

acceptedAnswer.text: Oil pump housing failure (catastrophic), turbocharger failure (often linked to oil starvation), and EGR valve/intake carbon build-up. The oil pump is the single most critical issue — address it preventatively or risk a full engine replacement.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Discovery 2.7 TDV6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Discovery 3 and early Discovery 4 values range from £3,000–£12,000 depending on condition. A £5,000–£7,500 reconditioned engine can be 50–70% of the vehicle's value on older examples — apply the Land Rover 60% scrap threshold. If the chassis and body are good, replacement is often worth it.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Land Rover Discovery 2.7 TDV6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is 276DT — it's the only 2.7-litre V6 diesel used in the Discovery 3 and early Discovery 4. You can find it on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, or on a label on the engine itself. Production years were 2004–2016.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 276DT oil pump, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — preventative replacement of the oil pump housing (£1,500–£3,000) is the single most important investment you can make in a 2.7 TDV6. It's a labour-intensive job but far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£5,000+). If the oil pump has already failed and destroyed the bottom end, a full engine rebuild or replacement is required.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Discovery 3/early Discovery 4 2.7 TDV6 market. This badge used one engine across two generations — see the Era Map above for generation‑specific pricing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Discovery 2.7 TDV6 engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer).

problems.title: Oil Pump Housing Failure (The "Crankshaft Killer")
problems.symptoms: Sudden engine failure, loud knocking from the bottom end, low oil pressure warning light, engine cuts out while driving

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: any low oil pressure warning or bottom‑end knock on a 276DT requires immediate investigation

problems.recommendation: This is the single biggest risk on the 2.7 TDV6. The oil pump housing has a weak tensioner mount that can fail, causing the oil pump to stop. The result is rapid bottom‑end destruction. Preventative replacement of the oil pump housing is the most important investment you can make in a 276DT-engined Discovery. If you hear cold‑start knock or see the oil pressure warning, stop driving immediately and have it recovered. A full engine replacement (£5,000+) is significantly more expensive than a preventative £1,500–£3,000 repair.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil pump failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger Failure
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, excessive black smoke, whistling or grinding noise from the engine bay, engine warning light
problems.recommendation: Turbocharger failure is a known issue on higher‑mileage 276DT engines, often caused by oil starvation (linked to the oil pump weakness above) or carbon build‑up. Replacing the turbo is a straightforward job for any JLR specialist. Ensure the oil feed pipe is also checked and cleaned or replaced. Don't ignore black smoke or loss of power — a failing turbo can send debris into the engine.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

problems.title: EGR Valve and Intake Carbon Build-up
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, reduced fuel economy, engine warning light, occasional black smoke
problems.recommendation: Like most modern diesels, the 276DT suffers from EGR valve and intake manifold carbon build‑up, particularly if the vehicle is used for short journeys. Cleaning the EGR valve and intake can restore performance and fuel economy. Some owners choose to blank the EGR (where legally permissible) to eliminate the issue. Use a JLR specialist who understands the 276DT's intake system.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR valve failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Discovery 2.7 TDV6 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: The single most important investment — prevents catastrophic failure
No oil pressure = bottom‑end wrecked; replacement required
Straightforward replacement — check oil feed pipe
Simple cleaning or replacement — restores performance
Viable on a well‑maintained example; if 2+ fail, evaluate full engine health

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Discovery 2.7 TDV6? Check These First
buyIf: * Proven oil pump housing replacement history — the single most critical item on this engine
* Post‑2012 Discovery 4 build date (improved oil pump design) — the safest era for the 276DT
* Full service history with correct oil spec (5W‑30 or 0W‑30, always meeting Ford/LR spec)
* No cold‑start knock and no oil pressure warning lights — this indicates a healthy bottom end
avoidIf: * No evidence of oil pump housing work on pre‑2010 vehicles — this is a non‑negotiable check
* Cold‑start knock or any bottom‑end noise — walk away unless you're budgeting for a full engine replacement
* Poor service history or evidence of neglected oil changes — the 276DT is unforgiving of poor maintenance
* Any oil pressure warning light without a documented fix — this often means catastrophic damage is imminent

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The 276DT is a capable and torquey engine, but it has one catastrophic weakness: the oil pump housing. A Discovery 3 or early Discovery 4 with this engine must have proven oil pump history. If that's sorted, the engine is robust and dependable. If not, you're buying a time bomb. The post‑2012 revised oil pump design is a significant improvement, but even then, maintenance history is everything. This engine rewards diligence and punishes neglect.

cta.label: → Compare 2.7 TDV6 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Discovery 2.7 TDV6 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Oil Pressure — Any warning lights? Cold‑start knock? This is the single most critical check on the 276DT
2. Service History — including which generation (Discovery 3 or 4) and evidence of oil pump work
3. Turbo Health — Any whistling, grinding, or black smoke under acceleration?
4. EGR Performance — Smooth idle? Any hesitation or rough running?
5. MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures (EGR, DPF, or catalyst issues)
6. Coolant Condition — Any signs of oil in the coolant or coolant loss? (head gasket warning)
7. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
8. Transmission Link — The 6‑speed ZF auto is robust, but ensure it's not slipping (linked to engine torque)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — one engine code across two generations.
